title: Getting started with stateR
text: Dynamic functional connectivity analyses parcel resting-state fMRI time series sequence discrete brain states — recurring patterns whole-brain co-activation identified clustering methods k-means hidden Markov models. volume (window) scan assigned state label, producing time series like: sequence, natural questions : often state visited? (fractional occupancy) long visit last? (dwell time) likely transition state state B? (Markov transitions) stateR answers three tidy, pipeable workflow.

title: Getting started with stateR
text: three functions expect long-format tibble one row per subject per time point, plus grouping covariate columns want carry output:

title: Getting started with stateR
text: simulate five subjects, 40 time points four possible states (0–3):
code: set.seed(42)  n_subjects  <- 5 n_timepoints <- 40  tbl <- tibble::tibble(   subject = rep(paste0("sub-0", 1:n_subjects), each = n_timepoints),   group   = rep(c("term", "preterm"), times = c(3 * n_timepoints,                                                  2 * n_timepoints)),   time    = rep(seq_len(n_timepoints), n_subjects),   state   = sample(0:3, n_subjects * n_timepoints, replace = TRUE) )  head(tbl, 8) #> # A tibble: 8 × 4 #>   subject group  time state #>   <chr>   <chr> <int> <int> #> 1 sub-01  term      1     0 #> 2 sub-01  term      2     0 #> 3 sub-01  term      3     0 #> 4 sub-01  term      4     0 #> 5 sub-01  term      5     1 #> 6 sub-01  term      6     3 #> 7 sub-01  term      7     1 #> 8 sub-01  term      8     1

title: Getting started with stateR
text: nest_fo() computes proportion time points subject/group spends state: result state-nested tibble — one row per state, data list-column holding subject’s fractional occupancy (perc): work specific state:
code: fo <- nest_fo(   tbl   = tbl,   vars  = c("subject", "group"),   foVar = "state" ) #> Loading required package: janitor #>  #> Attaching package: 'janitor' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     chisq.test, fisher.test  fo #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #> # Groups:   cluster [4] #>   cluster data             #>   <chr>   <list>           #> 1 0       <tibble [5 × 3]> #> 2 1       <tibble [5 × 3]> #> 3 2       <tibble [5 × 3]> #> 4 3       <tibble [5 × 3]> fo %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   head(12) #> # A tibble: 12 × 4 #> # Groups:   cluster [3] #>    cluster subject group    perc #>    <chr>   <chr>   <chr>   <dbl> #>  1 0       sub-01  term    0.325 #>  2 0       sub-02  term    0.2   #>  3 0       sub-03  term    0.325 #>  4 0       sub-04  preterm 0.375 #>  5 0       sub-05  preterm 0.15  #>  6 1       sub-01  term    0.25  #>  7 1       sub-02  term    0.4   #>  8 1       sub-03  term    0.25  #>  9 1       sub-04  preterm 0.2   #> 10 1       sub-05  preterm 0.425 #> 11 2       sub-01  term    0.175 #> 12 2       sub-02  term    0.175 fo %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(cluster == "2") #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #> # Groups:   cluster [1] #>   cluster subject group    perc #>   <chr>   <chr>   <chr>   <dbl> #> 1 2       sub-01  term    0.175 #> 2 2       sub-02  term    0.175 #> 3 2       sub-03  term    0.125 #> 4 2       sub-04  preterm 0.2   #> 5 2       sub-05  preterm 0.175

title: Getting started with stateR
text: nest_dwell() computes mean continuous occupancy per state — average number consecutive time points spent single uninterrupted visit. Single time-point visits (dwell = 1) excluded, likely reflect noise rather genuine state occupation. sortBy argument critical — ensures observations ordered chronologically run-length encoding underlies dwell time estimation. Always pass time index column .
code: dwell <- nest_dwell(   tbl    = tbl,   vars   = c("subject", "group"),   foVar  = "state",   sortBy = "time" )  dwell %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) #> # A tibble: 20 × 4 #> # Groups:   cluster [4] #>    cluster subject group   mean_dwell #>    <chr>   <chr>   <chr>        <dbl> #>  1 0       sub-01  term          2.5  #>  2 0       sub-02  term          2.5  #>  3 0       sub-03  term          2    #>  4 0       sub-04  preterm       2.4  #>  5 0       sub-05  preterm       2    #>  6 1       sub-01  term          2    #>  7 1       sub-02  term          3    #>  8 1       sub-03  term          2.5  #>  9 1       sub-04  preterm       2    #> 10 1       sub-05  preterm       2.25 #> 11 2       sub-01  term          2    #> 12 2       sub-02  term          2    #> 13 2       sub-03  term          2    #> 14 2       sub-04  preterm       2    #> 15 2       sub-05  preterm       2    #> 16 3       sub-01  term          2    #> 17 3       sub-02  term          3    #> 18 3       sub-03  term          2    #> 19 3       sub-04  preterm       2    #> 20 3       sub-05  preterm       2

title: Getting started with stateR
text: clusters_markov() computes transition probabilities states. source state, counts every observed transition normalises total number transitions source — first-order Markov chain. Transitions labelled tag "source_target" format. inspect specific transition: Set remIntra = TRUE exclude self-transitions (e.g. state 2 → state 2), useful interested genuine state changes:
code: trans <- clusters_markov(   tbl      = tbl,   vars     = c("subject", "group"),   cVar     = "state",   sortBy   = "time",   groupBy  = "subject",   remIntra = FALSE ) #> Loading required package: glue  trans #> # A tibble: 16 × 4 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [16] #>    source target tag   data             #>     <int>  <int> <chr> <list>           #>  1      0      0 0_0   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  2      0      1 0_1   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  3      0      2 0_2   <tibble [4 × 5]> #>  4      0      3 0_3   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  5      1      0 1_0   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  6      1      1 1_1   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  7      1      2 1_2   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  8      1      3 1_3   <tibble [5 × 5]> #>  9      2      0 2_0   <tibble [4 × 5]> #> 10      2      1 2_1   <tibble [5 × 5]> #> 11      2      2 2_2   <tibble [5 × 5]> #> 12      2      3 2_3   <tibble [5 × 5]> #> 13      3      0 3_0   <tibble [5 × 5]> #> 14      3      1 3_1   <tibble [5 × 5]> #> 15      3      2 3_2   <tibble [4 × 5]> #> 16      3      3 3_3   <tibble [5 × 5]> trans %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(tag == "0_2") #> # A tibble: 4 × 8 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [1] #>   source target tag   subject group   n             tot nCount      #>    <int>  <int> <chr> <chr>   <chr>   <table[1d]> <int> <table[1d]> #> 1      0      2 0_2   sub-01  term    2              13 0.15384615  #> 2      0      2 0_2   sub-02  term    1               8 0.12500000  #> 3      0      2 0_2   sub-03  term    1              13 0.07692308  #> 4      0      2 0_2   sub-04  preterm 4              15 0.26666667 clusters_markov(   tbl      = tbl,   vars     = c("subject", "group"),   cVar     = "state",   sortBy   = "time",   groupBy  = "subject",   remIntra = TRUE ) %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(tag == "0_2") #> # A tibble: 4 × 8 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [1] #>   source target tag   subject group   n             tot nCount      #>    <int>  <int> <chr> <chr>   <chr>   <table[1d]> <int> <table[1d]> #> 1      0      2 0_2   sub-01  term    2               7 0.28571429  #> 2      0      2 0_2   sub-02  term    1               5 0.20000000  #> 3      0      2 0_2   sub-03  term    1              11 0.09090909  #> 4      0      2 0_2   sub-04  preterm 4               8 0.50000000

title: Getting started with stateR
text: three functions return state-nested tibble shape: shared shape makes straightforward apply downstream analysis (e.g. permutation tests ptestR) across three metrics without changing pipeline.

title: Getting started with stateR
text: vignette("markov-transitions") — deeper look transition matrix vignette("grouped-pipeline") — running statistical tests across states ?nest_fo, ?nest_dwell, ?clusters_markov

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: practice, brain state analyses involve just computing metrics testing whether metrics differ groups associate continuous variables — across every state simultaneously. vignette shows full pipeline: Compute nest_fo(), nest_dwell(), clusters_markov() Unnest filter one state (transition) time Apply statistical model — demonstrated base R, compatible ptestR permutation tests

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: simulate 20 subjects (10 term, 10 preterm), 80 time points five possible states (0–4). States drawn unequal probabilities create realistic differences:
code: set.seed(2024)  n_sub <- 20 n_tp  <- 80  # Preterm subjects spend more time in state 0, term in state 2 state_probs <- list(   term    = c(0.15, 0.20, 0.35, 0.15, 0.15),   preterm = c(0.35, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15, 0.15) )  tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(seq_len(n_sub), function(i) {   grp   <- ifelse(i <= 10, "term", "preterm")   probs <- state_probs[[grp]]   tibble::tibble(     subject = sprintf("sub-%02d", i),     group   = grp,     pma     = rnorm(1, mean = ifelse(grp == "term", 40, 34), sd = 2),     time    = seq_len(n_tp),     state   = sample(0:4, n_tp, replace = TRUE, prob = probs)   ) })  dplyr::count(tbl, group) #> # A tibble: 2 × 2 #>   group       n #>   <chr>   <int> #> 1 preterm   800 #> 2 term      800

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: row state; data list-column holds one row per subject perc value covariates passed via vars.
code: fo <- nest_fo(   tbl   = tbl,   vars  = c("subject", "group", "pma"),   foVar = "state" ) #> Loading required package: janitor #>  #> Attaching package: 'janitor' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     chisq.test, fisher.test  fo #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #> # Groups:   cluster [5] #>   cluster data              #>   <chr>   <list>            #> 1 0       <tibble [20 × 4]> #> 2 1       <tibble [20 × 4]> #> 3 2       <tibble [20 × 4]> #> 4 3       <tibble [20 × 4]> #> 5 4       <tibble [20 × 4]>

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: Unnesting gives flat data frame per state. can map model across states using purrr::map(). use simple linear model (lm) test whether fractional occupancy differs group — swap ptestR::grouped_perm_glm() permutation test: Apply FDR correction across states:
code: fo_results <- fo %>%   dplyr::mutate(     model = purrr::map(data, ~ lm(perc ~ group + pma, data = .x)),     tidy  = purrr::map(model, broom::tidy)   ) %>%   dplyr::select(cluster, tidy) %>%   tidyr::unnest(tidy) %>%   dplyr::filter(term == "groupterm") %>%   dplyr::select(cluster, estimate, statistic, p.value) %>%   dplyr::arrange(p.value)  fo_results #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #> # Groups:   cluster [5] #>   cluster estimate statistic    p.value #>   <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 0       -0.177      -7.12  0.00000172 #> 2 2        0.172       4.88  0.000142   #> 3 1       -0.0164     -0.590 0.563      #> 4 3        0.0150      0.479 0.638      #> 5 4        0.00651     0.229 0.822 fo_results %>%   dplyr::mutate(p.fdr = p.adjust(p.value, method = "fdr")) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #> # Groups:   cluster [5] #>   cluster estimate statistic    p.value      p.fdr #>   <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 0       -0.177      -7.12  0.00000172 0.00000172 #> 2 2        0.172       4.88  0.000142   0.000142   #> 3 1       -0.0164     -0.590 0.563      0.563      #> 4 3        0.0150      0.479 0.638      0.638      #> 5 4        0.00651     0.229 0.822      0.822

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: pattern works dwell time:
code: dwell <- nest_dwell(   tbl    = tbl,   vars   = c("subject", "group", "pma"),   foVar  = "state",   sortBy = "time" )  dwell %>%   dplyr::mutate(     model = purrr::map(data, ~ lm(mean_dwell ~ group + pma, data = .x)),     tidy  = purrr::map(model, broom::tidy)   ) %>%   dplyr::select(cluster, tidy) %>%   tidyr::unnest(tidy) %>%   dplyr::filter(term == "groupterm") %>%   dplyr::select(cluster, estimate, statistic, p.value) #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #> # Groups:   cluster [5] #>   cluster estimate statistic p.value #>   <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 0         -0.133    -0.376   0.712 #> 2 1         -0.192    -0.526   0.606 #> 3 2          0.216     0.847   0.412 #> 4 3         -0.831    -1.56    0.140 #> 5 4         -0.490    -1.35    0.201

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: transitions, nesting key tag rather cluster:
code: trans <- clusters_markov(   tbl      = tbl,   vars     = c("subject", "group", "pma"),   cVar     = "state",   sortBy   = "time",   groupBy  = "subject",   remIntra = TRUE ) #> Loading required package: glue  trans_results <- trans %>%   dplyr::mutate(     model = purrr::map(data, ~ lm(nCount ~ group + pma, data = .x)),     tidy  = purrr::map(model, broom::tidy)   ) %>%   dplyr::select(tag, tidy) %>%   tidyr::unnest(tidy) %>%   dplyr::filter(term == "groupterm") %>%   dplyr::select(tag, estimate, statistic, p.value) %>%   dplyr::arrange(p.value) #> Adding missing grouping variables: `source`, `target` #> Adding missing grouping variables: `source`, `target`  head(trans_results, 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 6 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [10] #>    source target tag   estimate statistic   p.value #>     <int>  <int> <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> #>  1      0      2 0_2      0.312      5.26 0.0000638 #>  2      2      0 2_0     -0.337     -4.23 0.000638  #>  3      1      0 1_0     -0.251     -2.80 0.0123    #>  4      3      0 3_0     -0.181     -2.65 0.0181    #>  5      0      1 0_1     -0.153     -2.28 0.0359    #>  6      4      0 4_0     -0.226     -2.27 0.0372    #>  7      0      4 0_4     -0.108     -2.21 0.0417    #>  8      1      2 1_2      0.143      2.16 0.0486    #>  9      1      3 1_3      0.186      2.01 0.0614    #> 10      4      1 4_1      0.117      1.70 0.108

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: dHCP neonatal study, stateR outputs passed directly ptestR permutation tests 10 000 permutations. swap one line: pattern applies nest_dwell() (replace perc mean_dwell) clusters_markov() (replace perc nCount cluster tag).
code: library(ptestR)  fo %>%   dplyr::mutate(     res = purrr::map(data, ~ grouped_perm_glm(       tbl         = .x,       formla      = perc ~ group + pma,       var_to_perm = "perc",       permNum     = 10000,       seed        = 42     ))   ) %>%   dplyr::select(cluster, res) %>%   tidyr::unnest(res) %>%   dplyr::filter(term == "groupterm") %>%   dplyr::mutate(p.fdr = p.adjust(p.perm, method = "fdr"))

what: Notes on vars and covariate carriage
title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: column passed vars preserved data list-column therefore available inside every map() call. Covariates like pma, sex, motion_outliers included vars plan use downstream model — need special handling. One caution: vars nest_fo() nest_dwell() defines grouping metric computation. include continuous covariate varies within subject (e.g. time-varying measure), unique combination vars values become group. subject-level covariates (one value per subject) problem.

title: Grouped pipeline: testing across states
text: vignette("getting-started") — introduction three metrics vignette("markov-transitions") — transition matrix depth ptestR — permutation tests

title: Markov transition probabilities
text: first-order Markov chain models brain state sequence memoryless process: probability entering state jj time t+1t+1 depends current state ii time tt, earlier history. clusters_markov() estimates transition probabilities empirically observed state sequences. subject (group), : Lags state sequence one time point create source–target pairs. Counts every observed source → target transition. Normalises total number transitions source state, giving row-stochastic estimate transition matrix. result captures states brain tends move , probable transition — information fractional occupancy alone provide.

title: Markov transition probabilities
code: set.seed(99)  tbl <- tibble::tibble(   subject = rep(paste0("sub-", 1:8), each = 60),   group   = rep(c("term", "preterm"), each = 4 * 60),   time    = rep(seq_len(60), 8),   state   = sample(0:3, 8 * 60, replace = TRUE,                    prob = c(0.35, 0.25, 0.25, 0.15)) )

title: Markov transition probabilities
text: output nested tag — one row per unique source–target pair. Unnest see per-subject probabilities:
code: trans <- clusters_markov(   tbl      = tbl,   vars     = c("subject", "group"),   cVar     = "state",   sortBy   = "time",   groupBy  = "subject",   remIntra = FALSE ) #> Loading required package: glue  trans #> # A tibble: 16 × 4 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [16] #>    source target tag   data             #>     <int>  <int> <chr> <list>           #>  1      0      0 0_0   <tibble [8 × 5]> #>  2      0      1 0_1   <tibble [8 × 5]> #>  3      0      2 0_2   <tibble [8 × 5]> #>  4      0      3 0_3   <tibble [7 × 5]> #>  5      1      0 1_0   <tibble [8 × 5]> #>  6      1      1 1_1   <tibble [8 × 5]> #>  7      1      2 1_2   <tibble [8 × 5]> #>  8      1      3 1_3   <tibble [7 × 5]> #>  9      2      0 2_0   <tibble [8 × 5]> #> 10      2      1 2_1   <tibble [8 × 5]> #> 11      2      2 2_2   <tibble [7 × 5]> #> 12      2      3 2_3   <tibble [8 × 5]> #> 13      3      0 3_0   <tibble [8 × 5]> #> 14      3      1 3_1   <tibble [8 × 5]> #> 15      3      2 3_2   <tibble [7 × 5]> #> 16      3      3 3_3   <tibble [7 × 5]> trans_long <- trans %>%   tidyr::unnest(data)  trans_long %>%   dplyr::select(tag, source, target, subject, group, nCount) %>%   head(16) #> # A tibble: 16 × 6 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [2] #>    tag   source target subject group   nCount      #>    <chr>  <int>  <int> <chr>   <chr>   <table[1d]> #>  1 0_0        0      0 sub-1   term    0.3333333   #>  2 0_0        0      0 sub-2   term    0.3600000   #>  3 0_0        0      0 sub-3   term    0.2500000   #>  4 0_0        0      0 sub-4   term    0.3809524   #>  5 0_0        0      0 sub-5   preterm 0.1250000   #>  6 0_0        0      0 sub-6   preterm 0.3333333   #>  7 0_0        0      0 sub-7   preterm 0.4000000   #>  8 0_0        0      0 sub-8   preterm 0.2631579   #>  9 0_1        0      1 sub-1   term    0.2777778   #> 10 0_1        0      1 sub-2   term    0.3200000   #> 11 0_1        0      1 sub-3   term    0.1875000   #> 12 0_1        0      1 sub-4   term    0.2857143   #> 13 0_1        0      1 sub-5   preterm 0.3750000   #> 14 0_1        0      1 sub-6   preterm 0.3333333   #> 15 0_1        0      1 sub-7   preterm 0.3666667   #> 16 0_1        0      1 sub-8   preterm 0.4210526

title: Markov transition probabilities
text: reconstruct full K×KK \times K transition matrix averaged across subjects, summarise nCount source target: row sums 1 (within rounding error): given brain state source, row entries probabilities transitioning possible target next time step.
code: trans_long %>%   dplyr::group_by(source, target) %>%   dplyr::summarise(mean_prob = mean(nCount), .groups = "drop") %>%   tidyr::pivot_wider(     names_from  = target,     values_from = mean_prob,     names_prefix = "to_"   ) %>%   dplyr::arrange(source) #> # A tibble: 4 × 5 #>   source  to_0  to_1  to_2  to_3 #>    <int> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1      0 0.306 0.321 0.262 0.127 #> 2      1 0.346 0.245 0.236 0.198 #> 3      2 0.419 0.270 0.172 0.160 #> 4      3 0.387 0.267 0.277 0.119

title: Markov transition probabilities
text: default remIntra = FALSE, self-transitions (state ii → state ii) included. reflect dwell time transition matrix. want study genuine state changes, set remIntra = TRUE: remIntra = TRUE, rows longer sum 1 diagonal removed remaining probabilities re-normalised inter-state transitions .
code: trans_change <- clusters_markov(   tbl      = tbl,   vars     = c("subject", "group"),   cVar     = "state",   sortBy   = "time",   groupBy  = "subject",   remIntra = TRUE )  trans_change %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::group_by(tag) %>%   dplyr::summarise(mean_prob = mean(nCount), .groups = "drop") %>%   dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(mean_prob)) #> # A tibble: 12 × 2 #>    tag   mean_prob #>    <chr>     <dbl> #>  1 2_0       0.491 #>  2 0_1       0.467 #>  3 1_0       0.454 #>  4 3_0       0.428 #>  5 0_2       0.376 #>  6 2_1       0.319 #>  7 1_2       0.309 #>  8 3_2       0.307 #>  9 3_1       0.303 #> 10 1_3       0.271 #> 11 2_3       0.190 #> 12 0_3       0.179

title: Markov transition probabilities
text: Transition tags follow "<source>_<target>" pattern. four-state system (states 0–3), 16 possible tags including self-transitions, 12 excluding . work specific transition interest:
code: trans %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(tag == "1_0") %>%   dplyr::select(subject, group, nCount) #> Adding missing grouping variables: `tag`, `source`, `target` #> # A tibble: 8 × 6 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [1] #>   tag   source target subject group   nCount      #>   <chr>  <int>  <int> <chr>   <chr>   <table[1d]> #> 1 1_0        1      0 sub-1   term    0.1333333   #> 2 1_0        1      0 sub-2   term    0.4666667   #> 3 1_0        1      0 sub-3   term    0.3333333   #> 4 1_0        1      0 sub-4   term    0.3125000   #> 5 1_0        1      0 sub-5   preterm 0.3043478   #> 6 1_0        1      0 sub-6   preterm 0.3333333   #> 7 1_0        1      0 sub-7   preterm 0.5714286   #> 8 1_0        1      0 sub-8   preterm 0.3125000

title: Markov transition probabilities
text: Two arguments control grouping clusters_markov(), worth understanding distinction: groupBy — variable used compute transitions. Lag pair operations performed within level groupBy. always subject (session) identifier, transitions never computed across subjects. vars — variables carried output. Include everything want available downstream analysis: subject ID, group, covariates. groupBy subset (equal ) vars.
code: # groupBy = "subject" ensures no cross-subject transitions are counted. # vars includes "group" so group membership is available for testing. clusters_markov(   tbl      = tbl,   vars     = c("subject", "group"),   cVar     = "state",   sortBy   = "time",   groupBy  = "subject" ) %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(tag == "0_1") %>%   dplyr::select(subject, group, nCount) #> Adding missing grouping variables: `tag`, `source`, `target` #> # A tibble: 8 × 6 #> # Groups:   tag, source, target [1] #>   tag   source target subject group   nCount      #>   <chr>  <int>  <int> <chr>   <chr>   <table[1d]> #> 1 0_1        0      1 sub-1   term    0.2777778   #> 2 0_1        0      1 sub-2   term    0.3200000   #> 3 0_1        0      1 sub-3   term    0.1875000   #> 4 0_1        0      1 sub-4   term    0.2857143   #> 5 0_1        0      1 sub-5   preterm 0.3750000   #> 6 0_1        0      1 sub-6   preterm 0.3333333   #> 7 0_1        0      1 sub-7   preterm 0.3666667   #> 8 0_1        0      1 sub-8   preterm 0.4210526

title: Markov transition probabilities

what: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: R package characterising brain state dynamics functional connectivity time series — computing fractional occupancy, dwell time, Markov-chain transition probabilities across states.

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: stateR provides tidy, tidyverse-compatible toolkit quantifying brain moves discrete functional states time. Given data frame state labels time point — typically derived k-means HMM clustering dynamic functional connectivity matrices — package computes three core brain-state metrics: Fractional occupancy — often state visited Dwell time — long state continuously occupied Markov transition probabilities — likelihood moving one state another functions return nested tibbles slot naturally grouped purrr::map pipelines, making stateR especially well suited multi-subject neuroimaging studies. package developed used : França LGS, Ciarrusta J, Gale-Grant O, Fenn-Moltu S, Fitzgibbon S, Chew , Falconer S, Dimitrova R, Cordero-Grande L, Price , Hughes E, O’Muircheartaigh J, Duff E, Tuulari JJ, Deco G, Counsell SJ, Hajnal JV, Nosarti C, Arichi T, Edwards AD, McAlonan G, Batalle D (2024). Neonatal brain dynamic functional connectivity term preterm infants association early childhood neurodevelopment. Nature Communications, 15, 16. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-44050-z study, nest_fo, nest_dwell, clusters_markov applied resting-state fMRI brain state sequences derived k-means clustering dynamic functional connectivity 390 term- preterm-born neonates developing Human Connectome Project (dHCP), characterise state occupancy transitions relate postmenstrual age, preterm birth, 18-month neurodevelopmental outcomes (Bayley-III, Q-CHAT).

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: 🔁 Fractional occupancy — proportion total time spent state per subject/group (nest_fo) ⏱️ Dwell time — mean continuous occupancy duration per state, excluding single time-point visits (nest_dwell) 🔀 Markov transition matrix — pairwise transition counts normalised probabilities, optional removal self-transitions (clusters_markov) 📦 Tidy outputs — functions return nested tibbles, ready unnest() downstream statistical testing ptestR 🧩 Grouped pipeline-friendly — designed dplyr::group_by + purrr::map workflows across subjects, sessions, conditions

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
code: stateR/ ├── R/ │   ├── clusters_markov.R   # Markov transition probability computation │   ├── dwellCount.R        # Internal helper: run-length encoding of state sequences │   ├── nest_dwell.R        # Mean dwell time per state, nested by state │   └── nest_fo.R           # Fractional occupancy per state, nested by state ├── man/                    # Roxygen2-generated documentation ├── DESCRIPTION ├── NAMESPACE └── stateR.Rproj

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: R ≥ 4.0 following packages (installed automatically dependencies):

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: stateR CRAN. Install directly GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes")  # if needed remotes::install_github("circadia-bio/stateR")

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
code: library(stateR) library(dplyr) library(tidyr)  # Example: 3 subjects, 20 time points each, 4 possible states (0–3) set.seed(42) tbl <- tibble::tibble(   subject = rep(c("sub-01", "sub-02", "sub-03"), each = 20),   session = rep("ses-01", 60),   time    = rep(1:20, 3),   state   = sample(0:3, 60, replace = TRUE) )  # Fractional occupancy fo <- nest_fo(tbl,               vars  = c("subject", "session"),               foVar = "state")  # Dwell time dwell <- nest_dwell(tbl,                     vars   = c("subject", "session"),                     foVar  = "state",                     sortBy = "time")  # Markov transitions trans <- clusters_markov(tbl,                          vars     = c("subject", "session"),                          cVar     = "state",                          sortBy   = "time",                          groupBy  = "subject",                          remIntra = FALSE)

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: Estimates fractional occupancy — proportion total time points subject/group spends state. Returns nested tibble one row per state. row contains data list-column holding tibble fractional occupancy (perc) per grouping combination. Example output structure: Typical pipeline:
code: nest_fo(tbl, vars, foVar) # A tibble: 4 × 2   cluster data   <chr>   <list> 1 0       <tibble [90 × 3]> 2 1       <tibble [90 × 3]> 3 2       <tibble [90 × 3]> 4 3       <tibble [90 × 3]> nest_fo(tbl, vars = c("subject", "group"), foVar = "state") %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(cluster == "2") %>%   ptestR::grouped_perm_glm(perc ~ group, "perc")

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: Estimates mean dwell time — average number consecutive time points subject/group spends uninterrupted visit state. Single time-point visits (dwell = 1) excluded mean. Returns nested tibble one row per state. data list-column holds tibble mean dwell time (mean_dwell) per grouping combination. Note: internal helper dwellCount() performs run-length encoding ordered state sequence. transitions persist single time point contribute mean_dwell. Example:
code: nest_dwell(tbl, vars, foVar, sortBy) nest_dwell(tbl,            vars   = c("subject", "session"),            foVar  = "state",            sortBy = "time") %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(cluster == "1")

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: Computes Markov transition probabilities states. source state, function counts observed transitions target state normalises total number transitions source state. analysis memoryless (first-order Markov), treating transitions equally regardless history. Returns nested tibble one row per unique source–target transition tag (e.g. "0_2"). data list-column contains normalised transition probabilities (nCount) raw counts (n) per grouping combination. Transition tag format: "<source>_<target>" — example, "2_0" denotes transition state 2 state 0. Example:
code: clusters_markov(tbl, vars, cVar, sortBy, groupBy, remIntra = FALSE) clusters_markov(tbl,                 vars     = c("subject", "group"),                 cVar     = "state",                 sortBy   = "time",                 groupBy  = "subject",                 remIntra = TRUE) %>%   tidyr::unnest(data) %>%   dplyr::filter(tag == "0_2") %>%   ptestR::grouped_perm_glm(nCount ~ group, "nCount")

title: stateR — Brain State Dynamics for R
text: three exported functions return state-nested tibbles — one row per state (per transition pair clusters_markov), data list-column downstream analysis: nesting structure intentional: mirrors output ptestR functions makes straightforward apply statistical tests per state per transition using purrr::map.

what: This function reports the likelihood of transition between different states. The analysis is performed in a Markovian manner, i.e., without memory and all states have the same relevance.' — clusters_markov
